title: SEGMENT 11 — Directed differentiation: making neurons, hearts, livers, and beta cells

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Directed differentiation: making neurons, hearts, livers, and beta cells
OK, so now you have a beautiful iPSC line.
It's fully pluripotent.
It can become anything.
The question is: how do you make it become something specific?
You don't want a chaotic teratoma in a dish.
You want, say, dopamine neurons.
Or cardiomyocytes.
Or insulin-producing beta cells.
How do you guide the iPSC down a particular branch of the developmental tree, while keeping it off all the other branches?
This is the problem of directed differentiation.
And here's the elegant trick.
Development, in an embryo, is a sequence of decisions.
The early embryo has cells that decide ectoderm vs. mesoderm vs. endoderm.
Then those cells make further decisions — endoderm becomes lung, liver, pancreas, gut.
Each decision is mediated by particular signaling molecules — cytokines, growth factors, morphogens — that the embryo deploys at specific times and concentrations.
Wnt signals.
BMP signals.
Activin/Nodal signals.
FGF signals.
The embryo is, in effect, running a program of "add this signal for two days, then switch to that signal for three days, then add this other signal for a week.
Directed differentiation works by recapitulating that embryonic program in the dish.
You take the iPSCs, and you give them the same signaling cues that an embryo would give them, in the same sequence, at the same approximate timing.
Day one, you add this cytokine.
Day three, you switch to that one.
Day seven, you add another.
By following a recipe that mimics natural development, you guide the cells down the developmental tree to the lineage you want.
The analogy is: the reverse trick — give the iPSC the right cytokine cocktail and timing, and it goes BACK down the developmental tree toward whatever cell type you want.
Reprogramming rewound the tape; directed differentiation plays it forward again, but along the branch you've chosen.
Let me give you some examples.
Cardiomyocytes — heart muscle cells.

The recipe involves adding Activin A and BMP4 for a couple of days to push the cells toward mesoderm, then adding Wnt inhibitors to drive them toward cardiac mesoderm, then giving them time and the right metabolic environment to mature into beating cardiomyocytes.
The remarkable thing about iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes is that they actually beat.
You can look at the dish under a microscope and see the cells contracting rhythmically, dozens of times per minute, like tiny disorganized hearts.
They have action potentials.
They respond to drugs the way real cardiomyocytes do.
They are heart cells.
Dopamine neurons — the neurons that die in Parkinson's disease.
The protocol involves pushing cells toward neural ectoderm, then specifying them toward midbrain identity using sonic hedgehog and FGF8, then maturing them with neurotrophic factors.
After several weeks, you get neurons that produce dopamine, express the markers of midbrain dopamine neurons, and fire action potentials.
They are functionally similar to the neurons in your substantia nigra.
Hepatocytes — liver cells.
The recipe pushes cells through endoderm using Activin A, then to hepatic endoderm with BMP and FGF, then to mature hepatocytes with HGF and oncostatin M.
The resulting cells produce albumin, do drug metabolism, and store glycogen.
They behave a lot like liver.
Beta cells — insulin-producing pancreatic islet cells.

The recipe is one of the most complex in the field, taking several weeks and going through pancreatic endoderm, pancreatic progenitor, endocrine progenitor, immature beta cell, and finally mature glucose-responsive insulin-secreting beta cell.
Each step uses a different cocktail.
The final cells, in the best protocols, can sense glucose and release insulin in response — they are functional beta cells, derived from someone's skin.
The protocols for these differentiations are mostly worked out by a combination of looking carefully at embryonic development, trying many cocktails, and slowly optimizing.

Today, you can buy commercial differentiation kits for many of these cell types — order the kit, follow the protocol, and a few weeks later you have functional iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes or neurons in your dish.
But there's an important caveat.
iPSC-derived cells are usually immature.
They have the markers and many of the functions of the target cell type, but they're often more like fetal versions of the cell than fully adult ones.
iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes don't quite have the metabolic profile or the structural organization of adult cardiomyocytes.
iPSC-derived hepatocytes lack some adult drug-metabolizing enzymes.
iPSC-derived beta cells are not quite as glucose-responsive as adult human beta cells.
Maturing these cells fully — closing the gap between fetal-like and adult — is an active area of research.
Tricks like three-dimensional culture, mechanical stimulation, longer maturation times, and co-culture with supporting cells are all helping, but it's not a fully solved problem.
Still, even immature, these cells are extraordinarily useful.
They give you, for the first time, an essentially unlimited supply of patient-specific human cells of any type you want.
That capability transforms what you can do in research, in drug screening, and — increasingly — in the clinic.
